did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs) including EfficientNets, MobileNets, and DenseNets for the classification of skin cancer (melanoma and nevus) using the ISIC2018 dataset. It employs machine learning techniques such as categorical weighting domination, loss function optimization, and data augmentation to improve classification performance on imbalanced datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new approach for 'categorical weighting domination' to handle imbalanced datasets in skin cancer classification. It details specific contributions including feature extraction using DCNN backbones (EfficientNets, MobileNets, DenseNets), loss function optimization, and weight domination strategies. The study involves experiments on the ISIC2018 dataset, compares results against baseline methods (AU, FL), and reports quantitative performance metrics (recall, precision, specificity, F1), which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: Categorical weighting domination for imbalanced classification with skin cancer in intelligent healthcare systems
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of imbalanced classification in skin cancer diagnosis by proposing a categorical weighting domination approach to handle class imbalance in the ISIC2018 dataset. The authors utilize Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs), including EfficientNets, MobileNets, and DenseNets, to classify melanoma and nevus among other skin lesions. The proposed method optimizes loss functions and adjusts sample weights to boost minority class influence without relying on data augmentation. Experimental results demonstrate that this approach outperforms baseline methods like Focal Loss and standard data augmentation across various DCNN architectures. Specifically, the method achieved higher recall, precision, specificity, and F1 scores compared to existing techniques, particularly when using DenseNet and EfficientNet backbones. The findings indicate that balancing sample weights is a more stable and efficient solution for imbalanced medical image classification than generating new samples.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 7 categories in the ISIC2018 dataset used for multi-class classification. It is considered a minority class in the imbalanced dataset context.
One of the 7 categories in the ISIC2018 dataset. It is the majority class (head class) with over-represented samples, dominating the classification model.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin cancer diagnosis (Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis/Bowen's disease, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper addresses imbalanced multi-class classification of skin lesions using the ISIC2018 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (7 classes) using Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs) such as EfficientNets, MobileNets, and DenseNets. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics including Recall, Accuracy, Precision, Specificity, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset for skin cancer classification containing dermoscopy images from various dermatoscopy types, covering different anatomical sites and representing a historical sample of patients who underwent skin cancer screening across multiple institutions. It is highly skewed with the Melanocytic Nevus (NV) class being over-represented.

use.training: The original training dataset was separated into 80% for training and 20% for validation (validation2). It was used to evaluate and compare the proposed categorical weighting domination method against baselines like Focal Loss (FL) and Data Augmentation (AU).
use.validation: The original evaluation dataset was used as validation1. The 20% split from the original training set was used as validation2 to evaluate homologous characteristics and stable performance.
use.testing: Ground truth labels for testing images were not available as the dataset is used for a competition; thus, no testing split was used in this study.
use.other: Used to demonstrate the stability and efficiency of the proposed method across light and heavy DCNN architectures (EfficientNets, MobileNets, DenseNets).

datasets_used.name: Chest X-ray dataset
datasets_used.description: An imbalanced dataset comprising chest X-ray images used for additional experiments to assess the proposed solution's generalizability. It contains fewer categories compared to ISIC2018.

use.training: The dataset was randomly divided into distinct training and evaluation subsets with an 80% to 20% ratio. It was used to provide further results for assessing the proposed solutions.
use.validation: The 20% split from the random division was used as the evaluation subset.
use.other: Used to demonstrate that the proposed approach yields better results compared to CC and FL methods on three different backbone models, showing outcomes similar to those observed in the ISIC2018 dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute percentage differences in metrics (e.g., recall, precision, specificity, F1) between the proposed method and baselines (FL, AU) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC2018.description: Imbalanced skin cancer dataset with 7 classes (MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC). Training set: 10,015 samples. Validation set: 193 samples (original) + 2,003 samples (20% split of training).

Proposed Method (Categorical Weighting D.notes: Outperformed baselines across EfficientNet, DenseNet, and MobileNet backbones. Specific percentage improvements over baselines are reported below.

Focal Loss (FL).notes: Baseline method. Performed worse than proposed method on most backbones. On EfficientNetB1, it achieved the highest performance among baselines but was less stable than the proposed method.

Data Augmentation (AU).notes: Baseline method. Generated new samples. On MobileNet backbones, it was better than LF. On DenseNet backbones, it had significantly lower accuracy (8% and more) with DenseNet201/169. On EfficientNet, it was best for EfficientNet4 but worse for EfficientNet7/0.

Class Cross-Entropy (CC).notes: Baseline method. Achieved the worst results in all measurement metrics and backbones.

ISIC2018.notes: The proposed method achieved the state of the art on some criteria (REC, ACC, PRE, SPE, F1). It was more stable than FL and AU across different backbones. AU required higher computational cost. FL was not stable with all backbones (e.g., failed with MobileNets).

Chest_X_ray.description: Imbalanced dataset with 4 classes (Covid-19, Normal, Pneumonia-Bacterial, Pneumonia-Viral). Total 9,209 samples. Divided 80/20 into training and evaluation subsets.

Proposed Method (Categorical Weighting D.notes: Yielded better results compared to CC and FL on three different backbone models. Outcomes were quite similar to those observed in the ISIC2018 dataset.

Data Augmentation (AU).notes: Yielded better results compared to CC and FL on three different backbone models, but required higher computational cost due to increased training samples.

Focal Loss (FL).notes: Yielded better results compared to CC on three different backbone models.

Chest_X_ray.notes: The proposed approach supports improving performance without augmenting datasets, avoiding the high computational cost associated with AU.

architecture_used.main_model: Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs) including EfficientNets, MobileNets, and DenseNets
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning from pre-trained models using ImageNet for all backbones (MobileNet, DenseNet, EfficientNet), excluding the top layer

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Feature maps taken from the last layer of the backbone (e.g., 'multiply_56' for MobileNet, 'ReLU' for DenseNet, 'top_activation' for EfficientNet)
architecture_details.classifier_head: Custom fully connected network consisting of two dense connected layers with 1024 and 512 nodes, followed by an activated layer, a dropout layer with 50% probability, and a final output layer with 'c' nodes (where c is the number of classes) followed by softmax activation
architecture_details.optimization: Trial-and-error method used to estimate optimal architecture
loss_functions_evaluated: Data Augmentation (AU) - treated as a baseline method rather than a loss function

training_details.dataset_splits: 80% training, 20% validation (for ISIC2018); 80% training, 20% evaluation (for Chest X-ray)
training_details.augmentation: Data augmentation (AU) was used as a baseline method but avoided in the proposed method to prevent data overloading
training_details.hardware_requirements: EfficientNetB7 requires high hardware configuration due to large number of parameters

classification_or_output_head.structure: Final output layer with 'c' nodes (number of classes) following softmax activation function
classification_or_output_head.intermediate_layers: Two dense layers (1024 and 512 nodes), activated layer, dropout layer (50% ratio)

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not reported explicitly; Table 1 is referenced for parameter counts but values are not in the text

preprocessing_techniques: Geometrical transformations (image rotation, flip, skews, zoom, and crop) for data augmentation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used as a baseline method to balance the dataset. It involved generating new samples by applying image processing techniques to original images. Specific techniques included color processing and affine transformations such as image rotation, flip, skews, zoom, and crop. New samples were generated by random parameters in a predefined period, meaning each new sample was randomly created and fixed for all methods. This approach was distinct from dynamic image data generators (like those in TensorFlow or PyTorch) that generate new samples for each epoch; instead, the augmented dataset was fixed for all experiments to ensure objective comparison. The method was noted to produce a huge training dataset, causing overflow hardware requirements and significantly increasing computational time.

conclusions: The proposed categorical weighting domination approach effectively addresses class imbalance in skin cancer classification by boosting minority class weights and reducing the influence of majority classes, achieving high precision and stable performance without data augmentation.

The method outperforms baseline techniques such as Focal Loss (FL) and Data Augmentation (AU) across various Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) architectures, including EfficientNets, DenseNets, and MobileNets.
Specific performance improvements over Focal Loss include 2.73% higher recall, 2.63% higher precision, 2.81% higher specificity, and 3.09% higher F1-score using EfficientNet backbones.
Specific performance improvements over Data Augmentation include 5.16% higher recall, 5.97% higher precision, 8.93% higher specificity, and 6.16% higher F1-score using DenseNet backbones.

Data augmentation methods incur high computational costs and hardware requirements due to the generation of new samples, whereas the proposed weighting method avoids these issues while maintaining stability.
The Focal Loss method demonstrated instability across certain DCNN backbones, particularly failing to produce sufficient results with MobileNets, whereas the proposed method remained stable.

The proposed approach generalizes well to other imbalanced medical imaging tasks, such as chest X-ray classification, yielding results consistent with those observed in the ISIC2018 skin cancer dataset.
The study confirms that balancing sample weights is a more efficient and stable solution for imbalanced medical image classification compared to generating new samples via data augmentation.
important_remarks: The study uses the ISIC2018 dataset, which is a competition dataset where ground truth labels for testing images are not available; thus, no independent testing split was used, relying instead on training and validation splits.
The paper does not report any formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance comparisons are based on absolute percentage differences in metrics.

The proposed method avoids data augmentation to prevent data overloading and high computational costs, but this limits direct comparison with methods that might benefit from augmented data in different contexts.

The Focal Loss (FL) baseline method demonstrated instability across certain DCNN backbones, particularly failing to produce sufficient results with MobileNets, which may indicate sensitivity of FL to architecture choice.

Data augmentation (AU) was implemented by generating fixed new samples for all methods to ensure objective comparison, rather than using dynamic generators per epoch, which may not reflect real-world deployment scenarios.

The study includes an external validation on a Chest X-ray dataset to demonstrate generalizability, but this dataset is distinct from the dermatological domain, limiting direct clinical relevance to skin cancer diagnosis.

The authors acknowledge that the proposed weighting approach may have a 'corrupt problem when applied to imbalanced data,' though specific details of this corruption are not elaborated in the provided text.
The EfficientNetB7 architecture requires high hardware configuration due to the large number of parameters, which may limit deployment on resource-constrained intelligent healthcare systems.
